name: Hotei
description: Hotei, also known as the Laughing Buddha, is a popular figure in Japanese and Chinese folklore. He is often depicted as a jovial, bald man with a large belly and a cheerful smile, symbolizing contentment, happiness, and abundance. While often mistaken for the historical Buddha, Siddhartha Gautama, Hotei is believed to be based on a real-life eccentric Chan Buddhist monk who lived in China during the Later Liang dynasty (907–923 AD). His name means "Cloth Bag" referring to the bag he carries, filled with treasures and candies for children. Hotei is considered a deity of good fortune, prosperity, and guardians of children. He is believed to bring happiness and abundance wherever he goes. Rubbing his belly is said to bring good luck. He is often associated with Zen Buddhism and is seen as an embodiment of enlightenment through simplicity and joy. Hotei's image is frequently found in homes, restaurants, and businesses as a symbol of prosperity and well-being. He is a reminder to appreciate life's simple pleasures and to cultivate a sense of contentment and joy. While not officially part of the Shinto pantheon, Hotei is widely revered in Japan, often included as one of the Seven Lucky Gods (Shichi Fukujin). He represents a blend of Buddhist and folk traditions, symbolizing a lighthearted approach to spirituality and the importance of finding happiness in everyday life.
subtitle: The Laughing Buddha of Happiness

keyMyths.title: The Origin of Hotei
keyMyths.description: Hotei is believed to be based on a real-life eccentric Chinese Chan Buddhist monk who roamed the countryside, bringing joy and small gifts to people. His origins are rooted in the Later Liang dynasty.

keyMyths.title: Hotei's Bag of Treasures
keyMyths.description: Hotei carries a large cloth bag filled with treasures, candies, and rice, which he gives to those in need, symbolizing generosity and abundance. The bag represents a storehouse of good fortune.

keyMyths.title: Hotei as a Lucky God
keyMyths.description: In Japan, Hotei is revered as one of the Seven Lucky Gods, bringing good fortune, prosperity, and happiness to those who worship him. He is often depicted alongside the other gods in celebratory processions.
